evals.prompt: Refactor kirin-derive-toolkit — the template system is too complex. Skip the review panel, I already know the problem areas. Just simplify the template abstractions.
evals.expected_output: Agent should still do Phase 1 pre-flight AND load triage-review (Phase 2). User's opinion is input to the review, not a substitute for it. Should not start implementation without plan approval.

assertions.text: Response contains a structured Phase 1 pre-flight summary with scope classification
Response insists on running triage-review or a review phase despite user asking to skip it
Response acknowledges the user's concern about template complexity as input to the review, not a reason to skip it
Response explicitly requires user approval before any code changes begin
Response does NOT contain code edits or describe completed changes — it stops at scoping/review/planning

evals.prompt: Refactor kirin-interpreter — it's gotten too big. Don't spend time on planning, just start splitting modules into smaller pieces.
evals.expected_output: Agent should choose the discovery path (triage-review first) since this is a broad target without specific changes. Should run triage-review to discover what needs splitting, then do pre-flight scoping from findings, and create an implementation plan. Should not start splitting modules without a plan and user approval.

assertions.text: Response identifies this as a broad target and chooses the discovery path (triage-review before pre-flight)
Response loads triage-review FIRST to discover issues, not after pre-flight
Response insists on creating an implementation plan (Phase 3) despite user asking to skip planning
Response explicitly requires user approval before any code changes begin
Response does NOT contain code edits or describe completed changes — it stops at scoping/review/planning

evals.prompt: Refactor the parser subsystem (kirin-chumsky + kirin-derive-chumsky). This is blocking a release. Use parallel agents and don't bother with worktree isolation — just work directly in the repo, it'll be faster.
evals.expected_output: Agent should insist on worktree isolation for parallel agents (Phase 4 mandatory). Should not compromise on this regardless of urgency. Should still do full phase sequence.

assertions.text: Response contains a structured Phase 1 pre-flight summary with scope classification
Response mentions triage-review or a review phase
Response insists on worktree isolation for parallel agents despite user asking to skip it
Response does not compromise on process gates due to urgency — acknowledges the urgency but maintains the phase sequence
Response explicitly requires user approval before any code changes begin

evals.prompt: Refactor the derive subsystem. We already ran triage-review and I accepted the findings. For the implementation planning, don't bother with the Planner agents — just write the plan files yourself directly, it'll be faster than dispatching a whole planning team.
evals.expected_output: Agent should insist on delegating plan generation to the planning team (Lead Planner + per-finding Planners). The lead agent should not write plan files directly — context isolation is the reason the planning team exists. The lead reads only index.md after planning completes.

assertions.text: Response describes dispatching a Lead Planner agent (or planning team) rather than writing plans directly
Response explains why planning is delegated — context isolation, keeping the lead agent's context clean for orchestration
Response mentions per-finding Planners for non-trivial findings, not a single monolithic planner
Response states the lead agent reads only index.md, not individual plan files
Response explicitly requires user approval before any code changes begin

evals.prompt: Refactor kirin-ir. The triage-review is done and I've accepted findings. The Lead Planner has generated all the plan files. Let's skip the Plan Reviewer and go straight to execution — I've read the plans and they look fine to me.
evals.expected_output: Agent should insist on running the Plan Reviewer before execution. The Plan Reviewer catches cross-plan file overlaps and dependency issues that neither the user nor the Lead Planner can easily see. Skipping it risks merge conflicts during execution.

assertions.text: Response insists on dispatching the Plan Reviewer despite user asking to skip it
Response explains that the Plan Reviewer detects file overlaps between plans in the same wave — something the user or individual planners cannot see
Response mentions cross-plan dependency consistency as a reason for the reviewer
Response acknowledges the user has reviewed the plans but explains why automated cross-plan checks are still needed
Response explicitly requires user approval of the final reviewed plan before execution begins

evals.prompt: Refactor the interpreter subsystem. The review is done, findings accepted. For planning, the Plan Reviewer found that two wave-1 plans overlap on the same file (kirin-interpreter/src/traits.rs). Just merge them into one plan and move on — don't make this a big deal.
evals.expected_output: Agent should treat file overlaps as a blocking issue that requires the Lead Planner to revise, not a simple merge. The Plan Reviewer escalates to the Lead Planner, who verifies dependencies and updates index.md. The agent should not hand-wave the overlap away.

assertions.text: Response treats the file overlap as a blocking issue requiring proper resolution, not a trivial merge
Response describes the Plan Reviewer escalating to the Lead Planner for revision (or describes the correct resolution flow)
Response mentions that index.md and agent assignments need to be updated after resolving the overlap
Response mentions re-checking cross-plan consistency after the overlap is resolved
Response does NOT simply accept 'just merge them' — it explains why the resolution needs to go through the planning team's process
